serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of angel hair pasta with scallops and truffle oil weighs approximately 250 g, comprising a nest of chilled capellini topped with seared Hokkaido scallops, seaweed flakes, and truffle oil dressing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, enriched (SR Legacy ID 20121) and Scallops, mixed species, raw (SR Legacy ID 15152), combined with olive oil (SR Legacy ID 04053) as component estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighting USDA SR Legacy data for cooked angel hair pasta (~55% by weight), raw Hokkaido scallops (~30%), olive/truffle oil (~10%), and seaweed flakes (~5%). The input record's per_100g values (energy 220 kcal, protein 11.2 g, fat 14.0 g, carbohydrate 14.0 g) are broadly consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium 320 mg/100g is plausible given pasta cooking water and seasoning. All values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central component entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pasta, capellini / thin spaghetti, cooked (GI ~45, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for thin pasta / capellini cooked al dente (GI range 40–50, glucose reference). The presence of protein (scallops, 11.2 g/100g) and fat (olive/truffle oil, 14 g/100g) further attenuates the glycaemic response below the pasta-only GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 35.0) / 100 = 15.75, rounded to 15.8. Serving carbohydrate = 14.0 g/100g × 250 g / 100 = 35.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg per 100 g; estimated 800 mg per standard 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid seasoned seaweed flakes, as a single serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium — one-third of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate (angel hair pasta, GI ~45) with moderate glycaemic load of 15.8 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycaemic load of 15.8 per 250 g serving is moderate; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–180 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose, as refined pasta raises blood glucose more rapidly than wholegrain alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (2.0 g/100g), dominant unsaturated fat from olive oil, lean seafood protein from scallops
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cardiovascular-friendly due to its low saturated fat content, high proportion of monounsaturated fat from olive oil, and lean scallop protein; however, sodium intake should be monitored as 800 mg per serving approaches the threshold for patients on sodium-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory unsaturated fats from olive oil; lean seafood protein; very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olive oil's oleocanthal content provides COX-inhibiting anti-inflammatory properties; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should verify that truffle oil is genuine truffle-infused olive oil rather than synthetic flavouring, and request minimal added salt to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Capelli d'Angelo Capesante e Tartufo is a refined Italian pasta dish served in Singapore's upscale dining scene. The dish features capellini (angel hair pasta), one of the thinnest pasta varieties, which is cooked al dente and then chilled before being tossed with aromatic truffle oil and extra-virgin olive oil. It is topped with premium Hokkaido scallops — either lightly seared or served raw — and garnished with delicate seaweed flakes that add umami depth and a subtle oceanic flavour. The texture profile combines silky, slippery pasta strands with the tender, slightly sweet bite of fresh scallops and the crisp, papery texture of dried seaweed. Nutritionally, the dish provides 220 kcal per 100 g, with a notable protein contribution of 11.2 g from the scallops, moderate carbohydrate at 14.0 g from the pasta, and 14.0 g of fat dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive and truffle oil. Saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100g. Sodium is 320 mg/100g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), moderated further by the protein and fat content. The dish is suitable for cardiovascular health due to its unsaturated fat profile, but patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size given the moderate glycaemic load of 15.8 per standard 250 g serving. It is not suitable for shellfish or mollusc allergy sufferers, and contains gluten from the pasta.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked capellini, raw scallops, and olive oil, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for thin pasta. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-weighted estimate is well-supported and internally consistent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries: cooked enriched pasta (ID 20121), raw mixed-species scallops (ID 15152), and olive oil (ID 04053), weighted by approximate dish composition. Input record values retained as consistent with this estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (8.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.5) are estimated from olive oil fatty acid profile per USDA SR Legacy; not directly measured for this dish. 3. potassium_mg (220.0) is estimated from scallop and seaweed component data per USDA FoodData Central; not directly measured. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component values exist but reliable composite weighting for all these fields exceeds available data confidence. 5. The dish is served in Singapore and is of Italian culinary origin; multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per Project Veritas multilingual rules for Singapore-served dishes. 6. Truffle oil used in restaurant preparations is frequently synthetic (2,4-dithiapentane in a neutral carrier oil) rather than genuine truffle-infused olive oil; this affects the anti-inflammatory and flavour claims. 7. Seaweed flakes sodium content varies significantly between seasoned and unseasoned varieties; the 320 mg/100g sodium figure reflects a moderate seasoning assumption. 8. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate for thin pasta cooked al dente from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of this composite dish may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion.
